title: The association between adverse childhood experiences (ACEs), bullying victimization, and internalizing and externalizing problems among early adolescents â¦
abstract: â¦ 2-year follow-up of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study (4.0 release)â¦ you or be mean to you online, in texts, or group texts, or on social media (like Instagram or â¦

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study baseline (2016-2018) through 2-year follow-up (ages 9-10 years at baseline)

FT_IC2.evidence: Bullying victimization measured as dichotomized any/no bullying (cyberbullying + traditional bullying combined) but not a specific smartphone/social media variable separately extracted. Cyberbullying assessed only as single-item: 'Have you ever been cyberbullied...on social media (like Instagram or Snapchat)?' but this is part of broader bullying construct. No separate smartphone/SM exposure metric. Per E6, cyberbullying alone without separately-measured phone/SM variable = NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models with two main effects testing ACEs and bullying victimization associations with mental health problems; interaction term for ACEs × bullying victimization on internalizing/externalizing outcomes. Table 2 reports β and 95% CI for main effects.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports β coefficients and 95% CIs for ACEs (e.g., ACE=1: B=1.92 [1.27-2.58], p<.001 for internalizing) and bullying victimization (B=1.28 [0.72-1.84], p<.001). Includes p-values and confidence intervals.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using regression and interaction testing; secondary data analysis of baseline to Year 2 follow-up with inferential statistics.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal of Youth and Adolescence 2024; published article, not preprint or duplicate.

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 NOT_MET, so FT_EC7 does not apply. However, if FT_IC2 were met: bullying/cyberbullying is not a pure demographic predictor; ACEs are psychological/environmental (abuse, neglect, household dysfunction) not purely demographic. E16 applies: Individual-characteristic predictors of outcome (mental health problems) with any predictor type = MET regardless of direction.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study 4.0 release, baseline 2016–2018, ages 9–10 years to Year 2 follow-up. N=8,085 participants.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures cyberbullying victimization (single-item: 'Have you ever been cyberbullied...on social media like Instagram or Snapchat?') and traditional bullying victimization (Peer Experiences Questionnaire covering overt, relational, reputational). Both platform-specific or social-context specific bullying measures.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models with two main effects testing ACEs and bullying victimization associations with internalizing and externalizing problems (mental health outcomes). Table 2 reports inferential tests with coefficients and p-values.

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports β coefficients with 95% confidence intervals and p-values. Example from Table 2: bullying victimization B=1.28 (95% CI 0.72–1.84), p<0.001 for internalizing problems; B=1.40 (95% CI 0.88–1.93), p<0.001 for externalizing problems.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD Study longitudinal data from baseline through 2-year follow-up. Secondary data analysis with regression and interaction tests.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Journal of Youth and Adolescence, 2024. DOI: 10.1007/s10964-023-01907-2. Not a preprint or duplicate.

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does NOT apply. Although the paper examines longitudinal associations, the predictor variables (ACEs and bullying victimization) are tested in relation to mental health outcomes (internalizing and externalizing problems), which are individual psychological/clinical outcomes, not demographic characteristics. Per rule (b), demographic/contextual predictors alone are required for FT_EC7; this paper includes peer victimization and adversity predictors, which are individual-level behavioral/clinical exposures. Additionally, the paper reports cross-sectional associations and includes psychological outcome variables.

title: Associations Between Adverse Childhood Experiences, Adolescent Screen Time and Physical Activity During the COVID-19 Pandemic.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: To determine the associations between Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs), adolescent screen time, and physical activity during the early COVID-19 pandemic. METHODS: Data (2016-2020) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study were analyzed. Linear regression analyses estimated associations between ACE score and screen time and physical activity in May 2020, adjusting for potential confounders. RESULTS: Of the 6749 adolescent respondents primarily aged 12-13, 81.6% reported a history of one ACE or more. In adjusted models, a higher ACE score was significantly associated with greater hours per day of screen time, with youth with â¥4 ACEs associated with 2.3 more hours of screen time per day compared to youth with 0 ACEs. In addition, the adjusted models found that a higher ACE score was associated with lower physical activity; youth with â¥4 ACEs averaged 0.8 fewer hours per week of physical activity and 0.5 fewer days per week of 60 minutes of physical activity compared to youth with 0 ACEs. Gender and race were also significantly associated with changes in screen time and physical activity. CONCLUSIONS: ACEs are associated with higher adolescent sedentary behaviors, particularly greater screen time, during the early COVID-19 pandemic (May 2020). Clinicians caring for youth exposed to trauma in the postpandemic environment should explore screen time and physical activity behaviors.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from 3.0 release, COVID RRR Survey 1 (May 2020), N=6749 adolescents aged 12-13

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures generic 'total screen time' composite across 7 modalities (gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing, streaming) summed together; no separately extractable smartphone or social media coefficient

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression analyses with ACE score as predictor, screen time and physical activity as outcomes; adjusted models reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports B coefficients with 95% CI and p-values for ACEs→screen time (B=0.44, p<.001) and ACEs→physical activity (B=-0.17, p=.048)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using ABCD baseline and COVID RRR Survey 1 data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics, 2022

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 NOT_MET (generic screen time composite, not smartphone/SM-specific); EC7 does not apply when inclusion criteria fail

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (2016-2020), sample of 6,749 adolescents from 21 recruitment sites across the United States

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures screen time as specific smartphone/digital media variable: self-reported hours of use on typical day including multiple-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing internet, watching/streaming videos/TV (excluding schoolwork)

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression analyses examining associations between ACE score (0-9 continuous) and screen time outcomes; adjusted models with multiple covariates reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: adjusted B = 0.44 (95% CI 0.26, 0.62), p < 0.001 for ACEs predicting hours per day screen time; youth with ≥4 ACEs averaged 2.3 more hours screen time per day vs 0 ACEs

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis - cross-sectional analysis from May 2020 COVID RRR Survey data, linear regression design

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Academic Pediatrics 2022;22:1294-1299, not a preprint or duplicate

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply - paper includes smartphone/screen time as DEPENDENT variable BUT predictors include individual psychological characteristics (trauma history/ACEs indicate psychological distress exposure), not solely demographic predictors. Also analyzes associations with multiple non-screen-time outcomes (physical activity).

title: The association between screen time and cognitive function in children: The partially mediating role of putamen volume.
abstract: OBJECTIVE: This study investigates the association between screen time and cognitive function and examines whether putamen volume, which decreases with increased screen time, mediates this relationship. METHODS: Using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study of nearly 10,000 children aged 9-10Â years, screen time was assessed through self-report questionnaires, and cognitive function-including working memory, processing speed, language, executive function, and episodic memory-was Æmeasured using the National Institutes of Health toolbox. Linear mixed-effects models were used to analyze the association between screen time and cognitive function, and mediation effects of putamen volume were evaluated. RESULTS: Screen time was significantly negatively associated with all domains of cognitive function. The volume of putamen partially mediated the relationship between screen time and executive function (Î²Â =Â -0.015, false discovery rate [FDR] pÂ =Â .027), processing speed (Î²Â =Â -0.012, FDR pÂ =Â .027), and language (Î²Â =Â -0.005, FDR pÂ =Â .027). CONCLUSION: Excessive screen time was negatively associated with cognitive function in children, with putamen volume involving in the association of screen time with executive function, language and processing speed. These findings could inform strategies to promote healthy screen habits and support cognitive function and brain development in children.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study data, nearly 10,000 children aged 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured by self-report questionnaire assessing time on digital devices including television, videos, video games, texting, and video chat. Qualifying as smartphone/screen measure per FT-IC2 (texting, video chat, and digital device time are included).

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects models with multiple inferential tests: screen time significantly negatively associated with executive function, processing speed, language, working memory, episodic memory (all p<.001). Mediation analysis with bootstrap approach (10,000 samples) testing indirect effects.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β values and p-values reported. Examples: putamen mediation on executive function (β=-0.015, FDR p=.027), processing speed (β=-0.012, FDR p=.027), language (β=-0.005, FDR p=.027)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional design using linear mixed-effects regression and mediation analysis examining associations between screen time and cognitive function

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article; published in Acta Psychologica 2026

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria: screen time is the independent variable (IV), cognitive function is the dependent variable (DV). Additionally, mediation analysis is reported (putamen volume as mediator), which per FT-EC7 exclusion criterion excludes this paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD study data from 5.0 release with nearly 10,000 children aged 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is generic composite including television, videos, video games, texting, and video chat combined (Methods: 'total digital devices'). No smartphone/SM-specific breakdown extractable. Falls under 'generic total screen time' composite criterion.

FT_IC3.evidence: Criterion contingent on FT_IC2 MET; FT_IC2 fails

FT_IC4.evidence: Criterion contingent on FT_IC2 MET; FT_IC2 fails

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear mixed-effects models and mediation analysis with ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Acta Psychologica, 2026

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 NOT_MET; exclusion criterion does not apply
